coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dot: An excellent biocompatible candidate for in-vitro cancer cell imaging and beyond

Bhattacharyya, Swarup Krishna
Ghosh, Suman Kumar
Mandal, Mahitosh
Banerji, Pallab
Das, Narayan Ch
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are an exquisite class of carbon allotrope that is already well nourished for their good biocompatibility, water-solubility, excellent photostability, and magnificent photoluminescence property. Doping strategy with heteroatoms is an efficacious way to modify the physicochemical and optical properties, making the carbon dots an exceedingly potential candidate. This work reports the fabrication and cancer cell imaging application of photoluminescent heteroatom-doped carbon dots by use of cysteine and urea as carbon, nitrogen, and sulphur sources through a straightforward and highly productive hydrothermal procedure. The fabricated luminescent carbon dots are spherical in shape, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization revealed key facts about the surface functional groups and chemical compositions of carbon dots. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) peak appeared at around 445 nm against the excited wavelength of 350 nm. Moreover, under the provided experimental conditions, all the carbon dots are non-toxic and safe. The cytotoxicity and the safety profiles of the carbon dots were found to be in the bearable range under normal in-vitro experimental circumstances. Cellular uptake was observed by the green fluorescence of carbon dots inside cells. Likewise, the carbon dots did not alter the cell viability of the normal glial cell line. Again, when treated with the carbon dots, there was no notable increase of apoptotic cells in the G2/M phase of cell cycle analysis that confirmed the imaging-trackable ability of the carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of the as synthesized UC-CDs; the inset is showing the lattice fringes (b) Particle size distribution histogram of the UC-CDs, (c) AFM images of U–C/CDs, (d) Height profile diagram of the fabricated U–C/CDs.
(a) Typical FTIR spectrum of fabricated U–C/CDs. (b) XRD pattern of U–C/CDs. (c) XPS surface survey spectrum of fabricated U–C/CDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of fabricated UC-CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Emission spectrum of UC-CDs in different excitation wavelength (c) The emission spectra of cysteine at different amount for the optimization of prepared UC-CDs.

In-vitro cytotoxicity study of UC-CDs. Viability of cells was measured using MTT test at doses ranging from 10 to 1000 μg/ml. Viability of SVG p12 cells after the treatment with different concentrations of UC-CDs is shown in the bar graph. The survivability of control cells set was assumed to be 100%. The values shown are the mean ± SD.

Fluorescence imaging representation of control and UC-CDs treated cells. The nuclei of the cells were treated with the dye Hoechst 33,342. The red colour encircled indicates that both the blue and the green colour fluorescence coincides with showing the permeability of the UC-CDs into the cell. Bright field images of both controls set and the treated set (100 μg/ml) have been shown. Images were collected under Magnification = 10×, Bar = 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Live-dead cell assay images: stained with Calcein AM (green) to identify viable cells and PI (red) to identify dead ones. Cell viability did not alter or decrease over concentration after 24 h of treatment. Magnification = 10×, Bar = 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Bar graph representation of live/dead assay. Percentage of live/dead cell count of control and treated glial cells. The data shown are the mean ± SD (n = 3, **P < 0.05).

Cell cycle analysis of control and UC-CDs treated cells. The cells were cultured in 60 mm tissue culture dishes and treated with UC-CDs for 24 h at a time, and no significant increase in the apoptotic cell count in the G2/M phase was observed after treating cells with UC-CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an exquisite class of carbon allotrope that is already well nourished for their good biocompatibility, water-solubility, excellent photostability, and magnificent photoluminescence property. Doping strategy with heteroatoms is an efficacious way to modify the physicochemical and optical properties, making the carbon dots an exceedingly potential candidate. This work reports the fabrication and cancer cell imaging application of photoluminescent heteroatom-doped carbon dots by use of cysteine and urea as carbon, nitrogen, and sulphur sources through a straightforward and highly productive hydrothermal procedure. The fabricated luminescent carbon dots are spherical in shape, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization revealed key facts about the surface functional groups and chemical compositions of carbon dots. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) peak appeared at around 445 nm against the excited wavelength of 350 nm. Moreover, under the provided experimental conditions, all the carbon dots are non-toxic and safe. The cytotoxicity and the safety profiles of the carbon dots were found to be in the bearable range under normal in-vitro experimental circumstances. Cellular uptake was observed by the green fluorescence of carbon dots inside cells. Likewise, the carbon dots did not alter the cell viability of the normal glial cell line. Again, when treated with the carbon dots, there was no notable increase of apoptotic cells in the G2/M phase of cell cycle analysis that confirmed the imaging-trackable ability of the carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent era, photoluminescence nanoparticles have drained great attention of researchers owing to their immense exploitation in the field of sensors, catalysis, electronic field, biotechnology and many more (Poushali Das, Ganguly, Agarwal, et al., 2019a; Poushali Das, Ganguly, Maity, et al., 2018b, 2019b; Poushali Das, Ganguly, Mondal, et al., 2018a; Du et al., 2015; Ren et al., 2015; Weng et al., 2015; Tiwari et al., 2015). Their appearances as semiconductor quantum dots, up conversion photoluminescent nanoparticles, liposome fluorescent nanoparticles and polymer fluorescent nanoparticles have already gained severe attention in the different types of application fields (Malavika et al., 2021; Mansur et al., 2016; Ray Chowdhuri et al., 2015; X. Zhang et al., 2012).

Since 2004, carbon dots have been attracting much interest due to their unique nature and properties; among them worth mentioning their excitation-dependent fluorescence property, good aqueous solubility, and easy preparation with adjustable surface functionality, and superior biocompatibility with negligible toxicity (Hu et al., 2021; C. Liu et al., 2012; Y. Liu et al., 2021). In comparison with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, the CD has many superior properties, which make it a promising candidate for numerous fields of application as bioimaging (Bhattacharyya et al., 2020; Hola et al., 2014), optoelectronics (Stepanidenko et al., 2021), catalysis properties (Bera et al., 2016; R Thara et al., 2023; Mei et al., 2022; Ghosh et al., 2022; Vu Nu et al., 2022) and sensing (Bera et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2015; Yoo et al., 2019), bacterial detection (Poushali Das et al., 2017), cell labelling (Shi et al., 2018). Surface modification and doping are the primary strategies for enhancement and improvement of the fluorescence properties of carbon dots (Li et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2017; Mandal and Parvin, 2011; Travlou et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2013). Nitrogen used maximum case as doping purpose for CDs because of its comparable size with carbon, high electronegativity and unique electronic configuration. The addition of nitrogen introduces altered electron density into the CDs and changes the electronic environment, ultimately modifying fluorescence property and quantum yield. Excluding nitrogen divergent type of elements are available which can be used in doping purpose such as boron, sulphur, phosphorus or otherwise their combination. Apart from these elements several other elements are also used for doping strategy which results the refinement in fluorescence enhancement along with shift in emission spectra (Palash Das et al., 2021; Carbonaro et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019). Heteroatom doping in CDs is multifaced and highly convenient technique which boost up the structural and electronic property results it in an appropriate candidate for diverge field of application (Baets, 2019; Nagaraj et al., 2022; Q. Wang et al., 2019; T. Wang et al., 2020; Zhao and Tan, 2021).

Cancer is one of the deadliest diseases in the world, making it a major public health concern. Cancer was the second leading cause of death in 2017, behind only cardiovascular disease, accounting for an estimated 9.6 million deaths worldwide and annually accounting for around 1.5 million deaths in the United States and 300,000 new diagnoses. (Shen et al., 2015). Till now, several advanced technologies for cancer treatment have been included in recent time; still, patients with lung, pancreas, and breast carcinoma suffer from low survival chances, suggesting major improvements and research are necessary for the cancer diagnosis method (Bhattacharyya et al., 2022; Kalpana et al., 2022; Prabhu et al., 2022; H. Zhang et al., 2022). Until now, no single treatment for all types of cancer tumors has been discovered. In addition, these treatment methods have their own pros and cons, which can significantly affect the patient's life, like hair loss, pale skin, and vomiting (Kumar et al., 2022). The vital challenge for oncologists is appropriately diagnosing the specific tumor and correctly identifying a drug with maximum therapeutic effect and minimum toxicity (Jahan et al., 2013). Therefore, our main target is to find the perfect drug for a particular tumor that will have maximum medicinal value with minimum toxicity and side effects. In recent decades, much attention has been paid to nanomaterials, specifically carbon-based nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene quantum dots, and carbon dots which dominate most of the biological research field by virtue of their superior and excellent properties (Gupta et al., 2016; Koutsogiannis et al., 2020; Metrics, 2017; T. Zhang et al., 2019).

Carbon dots have numerous interactions with the body; interactions at the cellular and subcellular levels ultimately determine whether these particles have positive or adverse effects. Carbon dots also interact with cell membranes. For nanomedicine to be both effective and safe, its physicochemical properties must be modified to enhance cellular targeting, uptake, and trafficking. The nanoparticles, like carbon dots, are uptaken by the cells by various mechanisms, including phagocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-dependent endocytosis, clathrin-caveolae independent endocytosis, and micropinocytosis (Behzadi et al., 2018). Recent studies employed AS1411 aptamer-modified CDs, demonstrating excellent cancer cell imaging capabilities. It was discovered that cancer cells efficiently absorbed the system at the cellular level (Kong et al., 2020). Researchers synthesized and modified a CD-drug complex with a greater cellular uptake than the drug alone. They have experimentally verified how the cytotoxicity of the complex impacts cellular viability (Li et al., 2016). Doxorubicin conjugated CDs, which were recently discovered, have improved biocompatibility and anti-tumor efficacy on breast cancer due to their higher cellular uptake (Kong et al., 2018). Research on the cellular uptake process of Surface-State Controlled Multicolour Cross-Linked Carbon Dots verified the colocalization of these CDs into distinct intracellular compartments for the identification of various analytes, such as metal ions and biologically relevant proteins (Hu et al., 2022).

The present study shows how a synthesized carbon dot prepared from urea and cystine can act as an anticancer agent for the first time. The as prepared CDs is named here as UC-CDs considering the raw materials (urea-cystine) used for the CDs preparation and also to differentiate it from the others CDs in a huge family of carbon nanoparticles. Apart from the unique synthesis route and achieving enhanced quantum yield, this work provides a new kind of surface functionalization. The as-prepared carbon dots are biocompatible and show excellent photostability without any sign of photobleaching which ultimately helps in long-term cell imaging and could also be applied for small molecule tracking and biosensing application. There are only a few reports where carbon dots are used for glial cell line SVG p12 imaging and corresponding cellular uptake study. This study showed that our ultrasmall carbon dots could efficiently breach the glial cell membrane and enter the cells by endocytosis without producing any cytotoxicity. UC-CDs with different concentrations did not cause a substantial increase in cell death by inducing apoptosis in the normal glial cell line. These UC-CDs did not decrease the viable cell count over the dead cells. So, these heteroatoms doped carbon dots system may play a crucial role where glial cell labeling and image-tracking ability are of utmost priority in the future.

Cystine, urea were acquired via Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized (DI) water was utilized in the entire inspection. MTT (3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) and Triton X-100 reagent were acquired from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. (SRL). The other reagents and the chemicals and solvent that are used for this experiment, like DMSO, 4% paraformaldehyde, Hoechst 33,342 stain, Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS), Calcein AM, Propidium Iodide, 70% chilled ethanol, and Ribonucleases have been obtained from the Cancer Biology Lab, SMST, IIT Kharagpur.

The N and S codoped UC-CDs were procured from cysteine and urea by way of thermal coupling in a facile single-step hydrothermal treatment. Shortly, 2 g of cysteine and 0.7 g urea were solvated in 20 ml of deionized water and this combination was allowed to be stirred for 20 min for the formation of a homogeneous mixture of solution. Later, the obtained solution was moved into a sealed polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave reactor for heating at 190 °C for 2 h. The solution obtained after the reaction put for centrifugation machine at 8000 rpm speed for 20 min to discard the nonfluorescent materials, as well as the clear supernatant was collected. Later, the collected supernatant solution was transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000) for dialysis with distilled water for three days to remove the impurities. After dialysis, the solution was freeze-dried using a vacuum freeze dryer. After summarizing all these procedures, the ultrapure UC-CD powder was acquired and stored at 4 °C temperature for characterization and application purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization or instrumentation of as prepared UC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized UC-CDs were analysed using FTIR spectroscopy studies, which were investigated using a spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, model-Spectrum-2, Singapore) with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 16 scans within the range of 500–4000 cm−1. We checked UV and photoluminescence for the optical properties investigation. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the produced UC-CDs was determined by a UV spectrometer (PerkinElmer, model-2 Singapore, Lambda35). Fluorescence experiments were performed on U–C/CDs aqueous solutions in a Fluoromax. For the morphological structure and detailed microstructure of the UC-CDs, a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan operating voltage 200 kV with filament LaB6) was used for this experiment. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of the UC-CDs was investigated by a VG Scientific ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (UK) with an Mg Kα excitation source (1253.6 eV) and a five-channeltron detection system. To evaluate the amorphous and interlayer spacing properties of U–C/CDs, an X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical High-Resolution XRD-I, PW 3040/60) was used. The cell images for this experiment were taken with a Nikon Ti2 inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Instruments Inc, USA). The flow cytometric analysis was done using a Becton-Dickinson FACS Caliber (San Jose, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro cytotoxicity of UC-CDs against glial cell line
ce:section.ce:para: The SVG p12 normal brain cells were plated in 96 well plates at 8 × 103 cells per well and left to adhere overnight in the incubator. After 24 h, the cells were treated with serum-free medium supplemented with varying UC-CDs concentrations and were then given 48 h to incubate. Once the incubation was over, cells were assessed by the MTT (Sigma Aldrich, USA) assay protocol. The optical density was measured at 595 nm, and the results were analysed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cellular uptake of UC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The glial cell line, SVG p12, was seeded at a viable concentration of 2 × 106 per ml onto the coverslip, maintained within the incubator and allowed to adhere. Then the cells were allowed to grow in serum-rich medium for 4 h. The serum-free media was then added to the dish and a UC-CDs concentration of 100 g/ml was applied to the cells. After incubation for 5–6 h, the cells were rinsed in cold PBS and fixed with. Finally, the cell nuclei were stained with 10 mg/ml Hoechst 33,342 after being rinsed twice with cold PBS. For estimation of the cellular uptake of the UC-CDs, fluorescent microscopy was used, and it was expressed in the ratio of UC-CDs to Hoechst intensity. Additionally, the bright field images were taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays and imaging of the live and dead cells
ce:section.ce:para: To check for cell viability, we used Calcein AM and Propidium Iodide staining. The colour of the fluorescence emitted helped to differentiate between live and dead cells (Calcein AM-green vs. Propidium Iodide-red, respectively). The glial cell line SVG p12 was seeded at a viable concentration of 2.32 × 106 per ml, sixty thousand cells per 250 μl in eight welled chamber slides. We supplemented serum-free media with various concentrations of UC-CDs (100 g/ml, 500 g/ml, and 1000 g/ml) and administered it to the cells. On treatment for 24 h, cells were washed three times and stained with Calcein AM and propidium iodide. Next, fluorescence microscopy was used to capture images of the cells.

The cell cycle analysis of the UC-CDs treated glial cell line, SVG p12, was performed by flow cytometry as per the described protocol. The SVG p12 cells were then seeded, grown, and treated with different doses of UC-CDs (100 μg/ml, 500 μg/ml, and 1000 μg/ml) to analyse their responses. Once the cells attained 80% confluency. The cell pellets were collected and suspended in chilled ethanol and kept at −20 °C overnight. Propidium iodide (10 mg/ml) and RNAse (100 μg/ml) staining solutions were used for the treated samples. To measure the different phases of the cell cycle, flow cytometric analysis was conducted (Calabrese et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and microstructure study of the as prepared UC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface network topology and morphological pattern of prepared UC-CDs were confirmed by HRTEM and AFM studies. The size and morphology-related information were estimated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The HRTEM images show that the fabricated nanoparticles that formed are spherical in nature with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1 a) revealed that the graphitic layers are at a 0.24 nm interlayer distance with narrow size distribution and uniform particle size. A statistical analysis was done of the haphazardly present 100 particles on UC-CDs surface and it is proved (Fig. 1b) that those maximum particles are within the 1–9 nm range. Here, in Fig. 1b the x-axis is divided into particle size ranges, often referred to as Bins or class intervals. The AFM image (Fig. 1c) was taken for better clarification of the morphology of the as prepared UC-CDs. The image displays the near uniform particle size distribution of UC-CDs. The depth profile diagram also shown in (Fig. 1D) confirms the size distribution of prepared UC-CDs.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 a) provide detailed information about the surface functionality of the UC-CDs. The peak at 3188 cm−1 attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration modes of UC-CDs. Peaks at 2970 and 1540 cm−1 contributed to C–H stretching and bending vibration. A strong absorption peak is found at 1580 cm−1 which specified the CO stretching bond. Else, these C–N stretching vibrations (1292 cm−1), CC stretching bond (1437 cm−1, 1380 cm−1), and the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–NH–C (1114 cm−1) all present here (Atchudan R et al., 2019; Bhattacharyya et al., 2020). Hence, it can be seen that the UC-CDs surface mostly embedded with hydrophilic functional groups such as –COOH, –OH. The XRD pattern of the prepared U–C/CDs shown here which exhibits a sharp absorption peak around 20.56o (Fig. 2b) almost like the graphite lattice, which suggests an amorphous structure of the UC-CDs surface as well as disordered carbon atoms and graphene (Bray et al., 2018). XPS analysis further provides information about the surface chemical composition of the U–C/CDs. The XPS study proved that the fabricated UC-CDs showed (Fig. 2c) predominantly four peaks at 284.0, 400.0, along with 531.0 eV and 163.1 eV revealing that the UC-CDs surface mostly contains carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen and sulphur which proves also FTIR result.

The nitrogen, sulphur peak contents prove the successful doping into the UC-CDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of cysteine and urea. The high resolution C1s (Fig. S1a, supporting information) spectrum mainly consist of four peaks at 283.7, 285.0, 286.8288.0 eV which was mainly attributed to the C–C, C–N, C–O and CO bonds respectively. N1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) revealed two distinct peaks at 399.5 and 400.0 corresponding to N–H and N–C bonds, respectively. These two nitrogen peaks were ascribed to the aromatic CN groups of the prepared UC-CDs corresponding to the pyridinic and pyrrolic types of nitrogen, respectively (Lan et al., 2010). For O1s high resolution spectra (Fig. S1c) it consists predominantly of two peaks at 530.0 and 531.8 eV was attributed mainly C–O and CO bonds (Jung et al., 2009). For S2p (Fig. S1d) the two-spin orbit state are located at 162 eV and 168 eV for 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 (Hakonen and Strömberg, 2018) respectively. These all results were consistent with the FTIR spectral results of the prepared UC-CDs. Further, the elemental analysis demonstrated that the UC-CDs [Supporting information, Table S1] mainly consist of C (68.12%), N (10.57%), O (19.91%) and S (1.40%), which support the FTIR results also.

The elemental analysis demonstrated that the U–C/CDs mainly consist of C (68.12%), N (10.57%), O (19.91%) and S (1.40%), which support the FTIR results also. The elemental composition of each element is shown in Table No S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the optical properties of UC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible and photoluminescence spectroscopy were used to evaluate the optical properties of synthesized UC-CDs. A shoulder peak appeared between 300 and 350 nm which corresponds to the n- π* transition of CO groups of UC-CDs. Besides this, another small absorption peak appears in the near far UV region at around 212 nm which is due to π-π* electronic transition of CC and CO bonds (Block et al., 2015; Meric-Bernstam et al., 2015). Here (Fig. 3 a), represents the UV–visible absorption spectra of prepared UC-CDs.

The broad emission window that is the excitation dependent emission behaviour is demonstrated here in (Fig. 3b) with the variation of excitation wavelength from 300 to nearly 400 nm. The maximum intensity obtained at 445 nm for the excitation wavelength 350 nm. With gradually increasing the excitation wavelength a gentle increase towards the red shift is observed, which clearly reveals the excitation dependent emission behaviour. Several types of functional groups associated with the surface of UC-CDs give rise to the different energy traps that lead to divergent absorption and corresponding emission peaks as well as the asymmetrical and irregular particle size of UC-CDs, which results in excitation dependent emission behaviour (Namdari et al., 2017). We also investigated the effect of cysteine concentration on fluorescence intensity. From Fig. 3c, it is clear that with increasing the amount of cysteine keeping the amount of urea the same the fluorescence intensity gradually increases. The fluorescence intensity is highest when the amount of cysteine is 2 g. That's why we decided this optimized fixed ratio for better result. This result indicates that fluorescence intensity depends preliminary on the concentration of fluorophore. We also calculated the quantum yield of fabricated UC-CDs in presence of cysteine with different amount like 0.5 g, 1 g, 2 g, and 2.5 g. But the quantum yield was highest for the ratio of 2 g of cysteine and 0.7 g of urea, lower for other possibilities (like for 0.5 g it was 6.5%, for 1 g it was 7.6% and when the amount of cysteine was 2.5 g the value of quantum yield was 5.9%). The calculated quantum yield of prepared UC-CDs was 9.7% by using 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%) as reference upon excitation of 350 nm wavelength (the amount of cysteine was 2 g here). That's why the preferred ratio of cysteine and urea was 2 g and 0.7 g for superior result throughout the experiment.

CDs can be defined predominantly by their tiny size and large surface area generally faced with some dominant issue related with stability like self-aggregation and settling. These self-aggregation and settling is a serious drawback for the preparation method of CDs. Hence it is a key challenge to form the stable uniformly dispersed CDs for the finest application purpose in biological field and many more. Because of that we have checked the stability factor like fluorescence stability under UV irradiation as well as pH effect on the fluorescence intensity along with zeta potential of the fabricated UC-CDs.

The photostability of the prepared UC-CDs was done under the UV irradiation for up to 4 h. From the graph it is clear that the fluorescence intensity remains more or less same throughout the experiment. There is no significant change of fluorescence intensity up to 4 h of experiment. In consideration of this result, we can conclude that the prepared UC-CDs possess adequate range of photostability which is beneficial for the biological application purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effects on the stability of UC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we have studied the pH effect based on fluorescence intensity of the prepared UC-CDs to check the stability of it. We did the experiment of different pH change within the range of 2–12. From the graph of this it is clearly visible that the FL intensity relatively low in the range of 2–4 in comparison of 5–10. Afterwards in between the pH range of 10–12 the intensity came in insignificant region. In consideration of this result, we can presume that the prepared UC-CDs are stable within the range of 5–10. In the whole range of 2–12 the highest fluorescence stability was for pH 7. Hence, we have done the experiment at pH 7 where the highest stability of prepared UC-CDs was there for the better result throughout the experiment.

The cytotoxicity of UC-CDs was assessed by the MTT assay protocol in the human glial cell line, SVG p12. As per the results analysed, the UC-CDs had no effect on cell survival. Both the cell viability and the cytotoxicity were found to be appreciable up to a concentration of 1000 μg/ml (Fig. 4 ). This result indicates that the cytotoxicity and safety profiles are within the acceptable range under normal In-vitro experimental circumstances and could be used as a non-toxic carrier for other experimental purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake by normal glial cells, SVG p12
ce:section.ce:para: We can conclude from the observations that under laser excitation wavelengths of 461 nm and 494 nm, the deep blue colour appeared in the cytoplasmic area (due to the action of Hoechst) and even in the nuclear region of UC-CDs treated (100 μg/ml) SVG p12, indicating that UC-CDs (green colour appeared) can pass through the cell membrane and enter the cells. These findings (Fig. 5 ) indicate that UC-CDs can mark cells and track images under experimental settings.

As per the cell cycle analysis result, UC-CDs with varying concentrations did not cause apoptosis of glial cells, SVG p12. The cells were cultured in 60 mm tissue culture dishes and treated with UC-CDs for 24 h at a time. Considering the G1 population of the control set, there is no significant increase in the apoptotic cell number after treatment with UC-CDs at varying concentrations (Fig. 8 ). Moreover, the G2/M phase determines the growth and the cell division phase, which did not show any significant increment in the apoptotic cell number compared to the control set. So, we can conclude from the analysis that UC-CDs at different concentrations did not cause a substantial increase in cell death by inducing apoptosis in the glial cell line SVG p12.

Here we report on a simple hydrothermal process for producing extremely fluorescent heteroatom-doped UC-CDs. The as-prepared UC-CDs showed spherical microstructure (diameter ∼ 3.5 nm) with uniform particle size distribution, which AFM further supports. The FTIR spectrum confirmed the existence of –COOH, –OH functional groups on the UC-CDs surface, which is consistent with the XPS spectrum. The cell cytotoxicity assessment was conducted on a human glial cell line, SVG p12, and found non-toxic up to 1000 μg/ml. The cellular uptake study was also validated against SVG p12 cells after treatment with 100 g/ml and 500 g/ml UC-CDs. A deep green colour appeared in the cytoplasmic area under a laser excitation wavelength of 461 nm, confirming that UC-CDs can breach the cell membrane and enter the cells. A cell cycle study ultimately suggested that UC-CDs with different concentrations did not cause a substantial increase in cell death by inducing apoptosis in the glial cell line SVG p12. These results suggest that these heteroatoms doped UC-CDs can benefit cell labelling and image-tracking ability.

Trisita Ghosh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Suvendu Nandi: Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Swarup Krishna Bhattacharya: Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Suman Kumar Ghosh: Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Mahitosh Mandal: Reviewing and Editing. Pallab Banerji: Reviewing and Editing. Narayan Ch. Das: Supervising, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
